abstract: Since the landmark 1998 UK mapping report first spotlighted the economic potential of cultural and creative industries (CCIs), their importance has been reaffirmed through the creative industries "sector deal" and the broader UK Industrial Strategy. This chapter examines how CCI policymaking narratives have been integrated into Local Industrial Strategy (LIS) pilots across two city-regions in the West Midlands and the North West of England. Utilizing discourse analysis, the study compares the social practices and rhetorical frameworks within these pilots, focusing specifically on how "the local" is constructed, the specific language used to define creativity, and the "boundary work" inherent in policy development. The findings reveal a complex web of contractual relations between national and local governments. Drawing on Paasi’s theories of regional geography, the analysis highlights the discursive practices and social interactions that define these city-regional borders. Ultimately, this research demonstrates the agency of place-based actors even within centrally mandated policy instruments designed to stimulate sub-national economic growth.
